stories.title: Korean Chef Jiho Kim to Open Flagship Restaurant at 21 West 17th Street
stories.excerpt: Korean chef Jiho Kim has leased 3,120 square feet at 21 West 17th Street in Manhattan’s Flatiron District for his namesake flagship restaurant, Jiho. The New American eatery with a Korean-inspired menu will take up 1,868 square feet on the ground floor and 1,252 square feet of basement space at the Flatiron location between Fifth Avenue and Avenue of the Americas, Commercial Observer has learned. Asking rent was $120 per square foot. Carson Shahrabani and Noam Aziz of Meridian Capital Group negotiated on behalf of Kim in the transaction. “Jiho has been searching for his flagship location restaurant, and it was a pleasure to finally find the perfect site for him after showing him spaces for years,” Shahrabani told CO. Kim is the founder of the West Village restaurant Joomak and Olly Olly Market food hall restaurant Ddobar in West Chelsea. Joomak is temporarily closed, according to Yelp, and DDOBAR officially closed its doors in March of this year. The midblock address at 21 West 17th Street is within Louie, a two-building condominium complex developed by Vinbaytel Property Development. The building, completed in July 2024, includes 21 full-floor condo units. Its second retail space, located on the north side of the block at 16 West 18th Street, was leased to chef Andrew Sargent for his first New York City restaurant earlier this month. Ripco Real Estate’s Ben Davis and Alex Svetlakou represented the landlord. A spokesperson for the team did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Chef Jiho Kim leases 3,120 sq ft at 21 West 17th, Flatiron Manhattan at $120/sf.
Kim's prior restaurants (Joomak, DDOBAR) closed; flagship represents repositioning.
Ground-floor retail demand in prime Manhattan submarket remains active.
stories.summary: Korean chef Jiho Kim has leased 3,120 square feet at 21 West 17th Street in Manhattan's Flatiron District for his namesake restaurant at $120 per square foot. The space comprises 1,868 square feet on the ground floor and 1,252 square feet in the basement. Kim's prior food concepts have closed, and this flagship represents a strategic refocus.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Signals continued retail/hospitality investment and foot traffic in Flatiron, which can support nearby residential demand, but does not indicate supply, capital, or demand shifts material to apartment development.

stories.title: New-Construction San Diego Apartments Score $53M Bridge Loan
stories.excerpt: Broadshore Capital Partners, LLC closed on a $53.2-million floating-rate senior bridge loan secured by Monroe North Park, a newly delivered 137-unit Class A multifamily community located at 3090 Polk Ave. in the North Park neighborhood of San Diego. The loan was completed in partnership with an institutional investor with whom Broadshore maintains an active lending relationship. The financing will facilitate the lease-up and stabilization of the property, which was delivered in 2025. It features institutional-quality construction, structured parking with EV charging capabilities, rooftop entertainment and co-working amenities. “San Diego is one of the most fundamentally sound multifamily markets in the country, and North Park represents exactly the kind of supply-constrained, high-demand urban submarket we want to be in,” said Broadshore CEO Bradford Howe. “This transaction is consistent with our strategy of deploying institutional capital into well-located, newly delivered assets where we have conviction in the collateral quality and the long-term fundamentals.” The post New-Construction San Diego Apartments Score $53M Bridge Loan appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Monroe North Park, 137 units, San Diego North Park, closes $53.2M bridge loan.
New delivery in 2025; Broadshore cites supply-constrained, high-demand submarket fundamentals.
Bridge financing confirms lender confidence in San Diego multifamily collateral quality.
stories.summary: Broadshore Capital Partners closed a $53.2 million floating-rate senior bridge loan for Monroe North Park, a newly delivered 137-unit Class A multifamily community in San Diego's North Park neighborhood, delivered in 2025. The financing facilitates lease-up and stabilization. Broadshore CEO cited San Diego as fundamentally sound and North Park as supply-constrained with high demand.
stories.impact: Validates San Diego North Park as a prime infill submarket for institutional capital deployment. A lender's willingness to finance new construction at competitive LTV post-delivery signals strong market confidence in absorption and rent growth in this constrained urban infill pocket, supporting continued development feasibility for similar Class A product in San Diego urban neighborhoods.

stories.title: Gellner Retail Team at Kidder Mathews Welcomes Eric Hagen
stories.excerpt: Kidder Mathews has welcomed Eric Hagen to the firm’s downtown Seattle headquarters as a member of the Gellner Team. Hagen joins Executive Vice President David Gellner, First Vice President Parker Ksidakis, and Client Services Coordinator Michelle Plafcan, specializing in the sale of retail investment properties throughout the Pacific Northwest. Together, the brokers have completed more than 210 transactions totaling over $742 million, representing a range of private investors, institutions, developers, and property owners. “Eric is a strong addition to an already high-performing team,” said Regional President of Brokerage Will Frame. “His market knowledge, energy, and commitment to serving clients make him a great fit for Kidder Mathews and reflect the kind of professionals we want to bring to the firm as we invest in top talent across the region.” Among the team’s transactions are the sales of Cordata Shopping Center in Bellingham, Kitsap Shopping Centers in Silverdale, Wilderness Crossing in Maple Valley, Hobby Lobby in Tacoma, and Trestle Station in Lake Stevens. “We’re excited to welcome Eric as we continue to build on the momentum we’ve created,” said Gellner. The post Gellner Retail Team at Kidder Mathews Welcomes Eric Hagen appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Kidder Mathews hires Eric Hagen; retail brokerage team has completed 210+ deals, $742M.
Team focuses on Pacific Northwest retail investment sales; no multifamily or residential signal.
Team hiring reflects strong commercial real estate market activity in region.
stories.summary: Kidder Mathews welcomed Eric Hagen to its retail brokerage team in Seattle. The Gellner Team has completed over 210 transactions totaling $742 million. The hire reflects continued strength in the Pacific Northwest retail market.
stories.impact: Not material to multifamily development. Retail brokerage team expansion signals healthy commercial real estate activity in the Pacific Northwest but does not indicate demand, supply, capital, or cost shifts affecting apartment development.

stories.title: Distressed Houston Office Property Hits the Market
stories.excerpt: JLL recently posted a listing for the 632,511-square-foot midrise office building at 5400 Westheimer Court, which sits on a 6.43-acre property less than half a mile west of The Galleria mall. Built in 1981, the nine-story building most recently was 100% occupied by Enbridge Inc. (NYSE: ENB) following the $28 billion acquisition of Houston-based Spectra Energy Corp., which closed in 2017. The Houston Business Journal reports that JLL’s listing said the building may offer a redevelopment opportunity within a premier infill location. The building was previously owned by PTAD Realty. The company began to struggle when its sole tenant, Spectra, was acquired by Enbridge, which then moved into another Houston office. Enbridge’s lease in the building ran through 2026, and the company continued to make rent payments until earlier this year. The owners defaulted as a result of the the move. Ladder Capital Finance was the lender. Rialto Capital serves as the loan’s special servicer. The post Distressed Houston Office Property Hits the Market appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: 632,511-sq-ft office, 5400 Westheimer, Houston, listed as distressed; sole tenant vacated.
Enbridge departure after 2017 Spectra Energy acquisition; owners defaulted on Ladder Capital loan.
Listing highlights redevelopment opportunity in premier Houston infill location.
stories.summary: A 632,511-square-foot office building at 5400 Westheimer Court in Houston near The Galleria has been listed as distressed. The nine-story 1981 building was 100% occupied by Enbridge following its 2017 acquisition of Spectra Energy, but Enbridge relocated, and owners defaulted on the Ladder Capital loan when the lease expired in 2026.
stories.impact: Signals ongoing office distress in Houston's suburban commercial corridors, which can accelerate conversion or repositioning opportunities. For multifamily developers, vacated or underperforming office near major employment clusters (The Galleria area) may present adaptive-reuse or ground-lease acquisition opportunities, though the 1981 construction quality and parking density likely limit immediate conversion to residential feasibility.

stories.title: Pine Pre Properties to Open The Yard Gym Timonium
stories.excerpt: Pine Pep Properties, LLC has completed the $2.3 million acquisition of 2001-2003 Greenspring Drive, a single-story, 12,170-square-foot industrial and warehouse building situated in the Timonium section of Baltimore County, from 2001-2003 Greenspring Dr, LLC. Patrick J. Smith, Nick Maggio, and Mike Ruocco of MacKenzie Commercial Real Estate Services represented the seller, and KLNB’s David Fritz represented the buyer in this sale transaction. Pine Pre Properties intends to open The Yard Gym Timonium, a boutique, performance-driven training center with locations nationwide, in the nearly 4,200-square-foot vacant space this fall. The building, which fronts Greenspring Drive, was delivered in 1978 and fully renovated in 2024. It is equipped with 16-foot ceiling heights and two dock doors. “With Baltimore Flooring Supply retaining its presence, occupying approximately one-half of the total building space, Pine Pep Properties will be the immediate beneficiary of steady income flow from a stable tenant,” explained MacKenzie’s Smith. “Equally as important, the new owner has acquired a highly visible and strategically located asset with plans to backfill the remaining vacancy with a fitness concept.” The post Pine Pre Properties to Open The Yard Gym Timonium appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Pine Pre Properties acquires 12,170-sq-ft industrial building, Baltimore County Timonium, for $2.3M.
Tenant Baltimore Flooring Supply retains 50% occupancy; remainder backfilled with The Yard Gym.
Reflects adaptive reuse of industrial real estate in suburban Baltimore.
stories.summary: Pine Pre Properties completed a $2.3 million acquisition of a 12,170-square-foot industrial/warehouse building in Timonium, Baltimore County, built in 1978 and fully renovated in 2024. Baltimore Flooring Supply retains approximately 50% occupancy; Pine Pre will open The Yard Gym boutique fitness center in the remaining 4,200-square-foot space this fall.
stories.impact: Not material to multifamily development. Reflects adaptive reuse of suburban industrial real estate for fitness/wellness uses, signaling neighborhood demand for lifestyle amenities but not indicating residential supply, demand growth, or capital availability affecting apartment development in Baltimore County.

stories.title: Manhattan Subleasing Tightens as AI Firms Fuel Office Growth in Q2
stories.excerpt: Artificial intelligence and technology companies have been driving demand for office space in Manhattan during the second quarter of 2026, helping to soften the city’s post-pandemic sublease glut, according to preliminary second-quarter data from JLL. Manhattan’s sublease inventory has fallen below 11 million square feet, according to JLL, a steep drop from the late-2022 peak of approximately 23 million square feet of sublease space available. That decline is the result of an increase in leasing activity by AI companies, JLL found (although law firms have taken a rather large chunk of space so far in 2026 as well). So far this year, AI firms have signed 21 deals totaling 719,200 square feet in Manhattan, a number that is on track to overtake last year’s total of 845,000 square feet. About 70 percent of the leases signed were in the Midtown South market, which is evolving into a “tech and innovation core” in the city. Some of those Midtown South deals included AI sales platform Clay relocating to 163,095 square feet at 11 Madison Avenue in March and AI-driven advertising firm Agentio taking 20,000 square feet at 295 Fifth Avenue, also in March. “Manhattan’s office market gained momentum throughout the second quarter as demand continued to concentrate in high-quality space,” Jamie Katcher, executive managing director at JLL, said in a statement. “The most notable development is how quickly the sublease overhang has been absorbed,” Katcher added. “Growth from AI firms, as well as financial services companies and law firms, is driving long-term leasing decisions, while landlords are increasingly pulling back available sublease space to pursue direct deals at substantially higher rents.” Overall, office availability across Manhattan dipped by 50 basis points to 12.7 percent on a quarter-over-quarter basis, according to JLL’s data, meaning there was about 2 million square feet less space available in the second quarter than in the first three month
bullets: AI firms signed 21 deals, 719,200 sq ft in Manhattan YTD; on pace to exceed 2025 (845k sf).
70% of AI leases in Midtown South; sublease inventory fell below 11M sf from 23M peak late-2022.
Tech-driven office demand tightening Manhattan sublease glut, stabilizing office market.
stories.summary: Artificial intelligence and technology companies drove Manhattan office demand in Q2 2026, signing 21 deals totaling 719,200 square feet year-to-date and on track to exceed 2025 total (845,000 square feet). Manhattan's sublease inventory has fallen below 11 million square feet from a late-2022 peak of 23 million square feet. About 70% of AI leases landed in Midtown South, transforming it into a "tech and innovation core."
stories.impact: Strong office demand and sublease tightening may slow office-to-residential conversion pipelines in Manhattan and reduce investor pressure to convert trophy office assets. However, concentrated tech hiring in Midtown South and continued downtown revitalization create robust white-collar employment clusters that support premium multifamily demand in adjacent neighborhoods (Flatiron, NoMad, Hudson Yards), justifying continued Class A apartment development in transit-proximate infill sites serving tech worker demographic.

stories.title: Target to Open Store at Medina Station
stories.excerpt: Target is preparing to open its sixth store in Mesa next month. The Phoenix Business Journal reports Target will open a 148,000-square-foot store in Mesa at 1517 S. Signal Butte Road. The Target store will include a CVS Pharmacy, Starbucks Café and Target Optical. The new store is expected to employ more than 190 people. The new Target will be the first retailer to open as part of the new Medina Station shopping center. The 64.5-acre mixed-use center, which is on the southeast corner of Southern Avenue and Signal Butte Road, is being developed by Scottsdale-based SimonCRE. Medina Station will total approximately 300,000 square feet of retail space at full buildout. Besides Target, retailers and restaurants like Dick’s Sporting Goods, Einstein Bros. Bagels, Boot Barn, Zara Nails, Cafè Zupas, OHSO, U.S. Bank and Hawaiian Bros have signed leases for the project. The new Mesa Signal Butte Target will mark the 51st Target location in Arizona. The post Target to Open Store at Medina Station appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Target opens 148,000-sq-ft store in Mesa at Signal Butte Road; 190+ jobs, first tenant Medina Station.
SimonCRE developing 64.5-acre, 300k-sf mixed-use center; anchor retailer validates project.
Strong retail demand signals healthy suburban Phoenix commercial and residential fundamentals.
stories.summary: Target will open its sixth Mesa location and first tenant in the new Medina Station mixed-use center, a 64.5-acre development by Scottsdale-based SimonCRE at Southern Avenue and Signal Butte Road. The 148,000-square-foot Target will include CVS Pharmacy, Starbucks, and Target Optical, employing 190+. The center will total 300,000 square feet of retail space at full buildout and includes Dick's Sporting Goods, Zara Nails, and other anchors.
stories.impact: Target's commitment validates retail demand in southeast Phoenix/Mesa suburban corridor and anchors Medina Station mixed-use development, which typically includes multifamily. Strong anchor tenancy and retail pre-leasing signal healthy market fundamentals for residential density around the site; developers should monitor the mixed-use plan for residential component and acquisition opportunities in adjacent infill submarkets benefiting from new retail traffic and job creation.

stories.title: NADG Lands $120M to Build Rental on Farmland in Palm Beach County
stories.excerpt: North American Development Group (NADG) has secured a $120 million construction loan for a multifamily development on agricultural land near Delray Beach, Fla. Wells Fargo provided the financing for a 476-unit development, called NUVO Delray Beach, at 9494 Ilumina Way, located just south of Atlantic Avenue, about a mile and a half west of Florida’s Turnpike. The garden-style project will rise on 33 acres, 12 acres of which will be reserved for outdoor recreation, including a bird sanctuary. Construction got underway this week and is expected to be completed by late 2028. Four years ago, the West Palm Beach-based developer paid just under $42 million for the parcel, The South Florida Business Journal reported. The development sits within Palm Beach County’s 22,000-acre Agricultural Reserve, a protected farming region. In recent years, public officials have approved residential projects on the land, though mostly single-family homes developments. Some developments have been met with criticism. Last year, farmer Richard Bowman proposed building townhomes and workforce housing on nearby land, spanning about 75 acres, which environmentalists and some county commissioners opposed, the Palm Beach Post reported. So far, no construction has occurred, per property records. A representative for NADG did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: NADG secures $120M construction loan, Wells Fargo, for 476-unit NUVO Delray Beach on farmland.
Parcel acquired 4 years ago for ~$42M; 33 acres, 12 acres recreation; delivery late 2028.
Development breaks zoning on Agricultural Reserve land; signals policy shift toward residential in Palm Beach County.
stories.summary: North American Development Group secured a $120 million construction loan from Wells Fargo for NUVO Delray Beach, a 476-unit garden-style multifamily development on 33 acres near Delray Beach, Florida. The parcel, purchased approximately four years ago for under $42 million, is located within Palm Beach County's 22,000-acre Agricultural Reserve. Construction began this week with completion expected late 2028.
stories.impact: Signals Palm Beach County policy aperture for converting protected farmland to residential; recent approvals for multifamily and mixed-income projects on Agricultural Reserve land indicate municipal willingness to balance preservation with housing growth. Wells Fargo's construction financing at scale (476 units) validates lender confidence in Delray Beach submarkets; developers should pursue similar Agricultural Reserve conversions and workforce housing sites as county incentivizes in-fill density to ease affordable housing pressure.

stories.title: SoHo Leads U.S. Trophy Retail Property Sales in Q1: Report
stories.excerpt: Manhattan’s SoHo neighborhood emerged as the country’s most active trophy retail market in the first quarter of 2026. A new report by boutique capital markets advisory firm Adirondack Capital Partners (ACP) tracked 14 trophy retail property sales in premier U.S. shopping corridors, totaling more than $704 million nationwide between January and March. The findings top off a dramatic two-year reversal in fortune for luxury high street retail properties after years of post-2018 pessimism. Of the trades followed by ACP, six occurred in SoHo, representing 43 percent of such transactions. Other destinations among the 20 corridors ACP tracks include Madison Avenue in New York City, Miami’s Design District and San Francisco’s Union Square. SoHo is “definitely the belle of the ball as it pertains to high street retail locations,” Michael Hunter Coghill, founder and managing partner at ACP, told Commercial Observer. Coghill expects the SoHo corridor to retain the report’s top spot by the end of the year, although competition from Palm Beach, Fla.’s Worth Avenue and Boston’s Newbury Street is fierce. The SoHo neighborhood’s more than $195 million in investment activity included 120 Spring Street, which traded in January for $18.5 million, or $8,043 per square foot, to a private Japanese investor for Birkenstock’s flagship store. The deal, arranged off-market by ACP, marked the highest price per square foot achieved nationally during the reporting period, and remains the highest in New York City so far this year, according to the firm. Cross-border, high-net-worth capital is driving the trend, according to the report, particularly from Japan. The same private investor at Spring Street was behind the $46 million Cartier building trade at 102 Greene Street in 2024, and Japanese accessories manufacturer Yoshida & Company paid $34 million in March to acquire its own flagship building in Williamsburg, Brooklyn. Other high-flying New York City retail deals inclu
bullets: SoHo leads US trophy retail sales Q1 2026; 6 of 14 deals (43%), totaling $704M nationwide.
SoHo dominates high-street retail investment; Manhattan Madison Avenue, Miami Design District, SF Union Square secondary.
Luxury retail market recovery after post-2018 pessimism validates NYC retail resilience.
stories.summary: Manhattan's SoHo neighborhood led the nation in trophy retail property sales in Q1 2026, accounting for six of 14 tracked deals (43%) totaling more than $704 million nationwide. Adirondack Capital Partners tracked transactions across 20 premier U.S. shopping corridors. Other top destinations include Madison Avenue in NYC, Miami's Design District, and San Francisco's Union Square.
stories.impact: Not directly material to multifamily development but signals robust capital flow into trophy retail assets in Manhattan, particularly SoHo. High-street retail recovery may accelerate ground-floor mixed-use development and discourage office-to-residential conversions in prime retail corridors; developers should prioritize multifamily projects that integrate premium ground-floor retail in SoHo, NoMad, and Upper West Side rather than pursuing pure residential conversions.

stories.title: Habitat for Humanity shifts focus to outer boroughs
stories.excerpt: A renowned not-for-profit organization is expanding its output into New York City’s outer boroughs. Habitat for Humanity is constructing a total of 179 co-op units across Brooklyn and the Bronx, the Commercial Observer reported. The separate developments represent the largest influx for the New York chapter’s pipeline. In Brooklyn, Habitat is building Constellation, a community that spans eight sites in East New York, Ocean Hill, Brownsville, Bedford-Stuyvesant and Rugby Remsen Village, Brooklyn Paper reported. Originally envisioned for 77 units, the organization has since grown the project out to 108 units. Similarly, Claremont House in the Bronx jumped from a 40-unit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Habitat for Humanity planning 179 co-op units across Brooklyn and Bronx; Constellation (108 units), Claremont House (71).
Projects leveraging City of Yes, Open Door Program, New Infill Housing Opportunity Program; Community Land Trust ownership.
Affordable housing pipeline targets 'missing middle'; City budget allocates $22B for housing.
stories.summary: Habitat for Humanity is constructing 179 co-op units across Brooklyn and the Bronx—its largest pipeline expansion. Constellation spans eight East New York, Brownsville, and Bed-Stuy sites (108 units, grown from 77) and Claremont House in the Bronx (71 units, grown from 40). Projects utilize City of Yes zoning, tax abatements, and a 99-year Community Land Trust ground lease model, targeting the 'missing middle.'
stories.impact: Signals NYC municipal acceleration of zoning and program reforms (City of Yes, Open Door) that will increase competitive supply of affordable co-op and community-land-trust units, reducing market for for-profit multifamily developers in underserved outer-borough neighborhoods. Developers should reposition toward higher-rent market-rate infill in Williamsburg, Astoria, and higher-opportunity submarkets, or pursue public-private affordable partnerships in less competitive secondary neighborhoods to preserve feasibility.

stories.title: Peachtree Supplies $56M C-PACE Loan for Conversion of Former CNN HQ in Atlanta
stories.excerpt: Breaking news: A conversion of CNN’s former global headquarters is underway with fresh long-term capital in hand. A joint venture between CP Group and Rialto Capital Management has secured a $56.4 million loan for the repositioning of the CTR building (previously known as CNN Center) in Downtown Atlanta, Commercial Observer has learned. Peachtree Group originated the 30-year Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) debt to support the transformation of the 1976-built property into a mixed-use campus and retail district. The historic building, which had sat vacant since CNN relocated its operations to the Ted Turner Techwood Campus in Midtown Atlanta in early 2024, debuted a new 24,000-square-foot food hall this spring to coincide with the kickoff of the FIFA World Cup. Jared Schlosser, head of credit originations at Atlanta-based Peachtree Group, said the deal was conducive to long-term debt like C-PACE due to the sponsors implementing a multiphase business plan at the 1.2 million-square-foot property that requires flexibility when building out. The C-PACE debt will be used to fund eligible improvements to the building including HVAC systems, lighting, plumbing infrastructure and LED media displays. “When you have a multiphase business plan like this where you need timing and flexibility, C-PACE becomes a really good product that not everyone’s thinking about,” Schlosser told CO. “Whenever you’re taking a building that’s as old as this and you’re repurposing it, you’re going to naturally have a lot of PACE-qualifying items.” Located at 190 Marietta Street NW and connected to State Farm Arena, the large-scale redevelopment project will involve activating 200,000 square feet of retail space in the property’s ground-floor atrium and installing some of the largest digital media displays in the Southeast. The upper floors of the center are slated to eventually feature office, hotel and residential use. Schlosser said the retail com
bullets: CP Group and Rialto Capital secure $56.4M C-PACE 30-year loan for CNN Center conversion, Atlanta.
1.2M-sq-ft historic 1976 building converting to mixed-use campus and retail; food hall opened spring 2026.
Long-term C-PACE debt supports phased multiphase conversion strategy.
stories.summary: A joint venture between CP Group and Rialto Capital Management secured a $56.4 million 30-year C-PACE loan from Peachtree Group to reposition CNN Center (CTR building) in Downtown Atlanta into a mixed-use campus and retail district. The 1.2 million-square-foot historic property, vacant since CNN relocated to Midtown in early 2024, debuted a 24,000-square-foot food hall this spring. The C-PACE structure supports a multiphase redevelopment plan.
stories.impact: Demonstrates viability of long-term C-PACE debt for large-scale office-to-mixed-use conversions in secondary downtown cores. Atlanta developers should accelerate similar adaptive-reuse projects on vacant corporate campuses and historic office stock using C-PACE, which offers 30-year terms and flexible phasing; this deal validates lender appetite for downtown revitalization and validates Downtown Atlanta as a conversion opportunity zone despite recent tenant defections.

stories.title: Houston Rodeo partially breaks from NRG Park with $300M livestock campus
stories.excerpt: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is partially bucking NRG Park to build its own $300 million facility about five miles east.  The Rodeo is building a 1 million-square-foot complex at the corner of Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard. Part of the project will be the construction of their own arena, but some operations will remain at NRG Park. The Rodeo is working on a new lease with the Houston Texans as well as Harris County, according to the Houston Business Journal.  Houston Rodeo president and CEO Chris Boleman told the outlet that this is not a relocation, even though […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo commits $300M to new 1M-sq-ft campus at Highway 288 and Airport Blvd.
New facility includes 860k sq ft barns, 140k sq ft central barn, auction hall; year-round event flexibility.
Main rodeo events remain at NRG; new complex enables expanded agricultural events and tourism.
stories.summary: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is building a $300 million, 1 million-square-foot complex five miles east of NRG Park at Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard. The facility will include two barns spanning 860,000 square feet, a central barn of 140,000 square feet, livestock yards, and a combination auction/convention center. Main rodeo events remain at NRG; the new campus will host year-round agricultural and livestock shows.
stories.impact: Signals major events-driven development opportunity in southeast Houston near Highway 288 corridor. The Rodeo's capital commitment and new event footprint will generate nearby hospitality, retail, and service demand; multifamily developers should assess infill opportunities near the Airport Boulevard corridor to capture long-term event-driven job creation and visitor-serving demand that will follow the Rodeo relocation.

stories.title: Developer looks to bring almost 300 units to Flushing
stories.excerpt: Flushing is poised to receive an influx of nearly 300 units — many affordable — should Gary Chen secure a zoning change. On Monday, Chen filed an application with the New York City Department of City Planning for a mixed-use project at 32-02 Linden Place in the Queens neighborhood, the Commercial Observer reported. The zoning change would be bound by 32nd Avenue, Linden Place and Farrington Street. Chen wants to build a 10-story, 296-unit complex at the site, which would also include 33,000 square feet of commercial space and nearly 200 parking spaces. Roughly 25 percent of the units would […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gary Chen files zoning change for 296-unit, 10-story mixed-use at 32-02 Linden Place, Flushing Queens.
~25% affordable units; 33k sf commercial, 200 parking spaces; site assembled 2024 for $26.5M.
Multiple Queens filings (Grun Group Kew Gardens 108 units, Derek Fraser Ditmars 26 units) signal pipeline acceleration.
stories.summary: Developer Gary Chen filed a zoning change application with NYC Department of City Planning for a 296-unit, 10-story mixed-use project at 32-02 Linden Place in Flushing. The development will include approximately 25% affordable units, 33,000 square feet of commercial space, and nearly 200 parking spaces. Chen assembled the multi-parcel site in 2024 for $26.5 million.
stories.impact: Flushing is experiencing accelerated zoning applications and multifamily pipeline growth, driven by Chen's strategic land assembly and emerging policy support (City of Yes). Queens developers should expedite zoning applications in Flushing and similarly transit-rich corridors before political/market cycles shift; strong early-stage filing activity suggests competitive entitlement windows and rising land costs as speculative capital flows to outer boroughs.

stories.title: Dallas secures $19M worth of incentives for potential Morgan Stanley move
stories.excerpt: Dallas City Council greenlit an $18.5 million incentive package Wednesday for Morgan Stanley’s potential Y’all Street move.  The financial services and investment bank will spend a little time in Downtown Dallas before moving to Uptown in 2031. The package coincides with Morgan Stanley earmarking $700 million for its new digs at 2401 McKinney Avenue, which will bring hundreds of employees to Dallas’s burgeoning financial hub.  Morgan Stanley has yet to formally communicate their plans, but documents filed with the city indicate the timeline, according to the Dallas Business Journal. Morgan Stanley can still opt out and head to Alpharetta, Georgia, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Dallas City Council approves $18.5M incentive package for Morgan Stanley Uptown move; $700M total investment.
Morgan Stanley leasing 255k sf downtown Fountain Place temporarily until 2031; permanent 700k sf at 2401 McKinney.
Trammell Crow contributing $650M; 3,800 jobs projected; construction begins fall 2026.
stories.summary: Dallas City Council greenlit an $18.5 million incentive package for Morgan Stanley's potential relocation, contingent on the bank's $700 million office investment. Morgan Stanley will temporarily occupy 255,000 square feet at downtown's Fountain Place before moving to Uptown's 2401 McKinney Avenue in 2031, where it will occupy 700,000 square feet and employ approximately 3,800. Trammell Crow Company is investing $650 million; total project value is approximately $1.3 billion. Construction is set to begin fall 2026.
stories.impact: Morgan Stanley's $700M office commitment and 3,800-job influx will fuel sustained multifamily demand in Dallas Uptown through 2031 and beyond. Developers should acquire and entitle Class A residential product within walk/transit distance of 2401 McKinney and Fountain Place (Downtown) to capture sustained white-collar migration and high-income renter demand; this represents a 5+ year developmental tailwind for Dallas apartment supply in Central Business District and adjacent emerging neighborhoods (Deep Ellum, Lower Greenville).

stories.title: Skyscrapers encouraged: Development near transit in California’s biggest cities poised for major boost from new bill
stories.excerpt: Transit-oriented development in major cities across the Golden State is poised to take a much firmer shape with a piece of legislation that could help municipalities close the gap between housing demand and state-mandated planning goals. Assembly Bill 2074, dubbed the Downtown Revitalization Act, is quickly working its way through the state legislature. The California State Assembly passed the proposed law in a 55-5 vote on May 28 and the State Senate amended and referred it to the Senate Housing Committee last week. Authored by Assembly member Matt Haney of San Francisco, AB 2074 would supercharge housing development in seven […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: California AB 2074 (Downtown Revitalization Act) passed Assembly 55-5; mandatory transit-oriented zoning in 7 cities.
$500M low-interest revolving construction loan fund; 150-ft minimum, 450-ft permitted in districts.
Applies to LA, San Diego, San Jose, SF, Sacramento, Oakland, Long Beach; expedited approval for labor-compliant projects.
stories.summary: Assembly Bill 2074, the Downtown Revitalization Act, passed the California State Assembly 55-5 and was referred to Senate Housing Committee. The bill mandates creation of dense housing hubs in seven major cities (LA, San Diego, San Jose, SF, Sacramento, Oakland, Long Beach), sets minimum heights of 150 feet with 450-foot-tall buildings permitted in designated regional transit districts, and provides a $500 million low-interest revolving loan fund to lower construction costs for developers. Labor-compliant projects receive expedited approval.
stories.impact: AB 2074 represents a material regulatory shift favoring high-density multifamily in California's largest metros. If enacted, the $500M state construction loan fund and ministerial approval pathway will dramatically reduce entitlement risk and financing costs for Class A apartment development near transit; developers should immediately position land and begin pre-entitlement work in LA, SD, SF, Oakland urban core sites to secure advantage before competitive capital floods these markets post-enactment.

stories.title: Charney, Tavros snag $785M financing for historic Gowanus project
stories.excerpt: A joint venture led by Charney Companies and Tavros secured a new package of debt and equity for their historic Gowanus development. Charney, Tavros and Incoco Capital landed $785 million in financing for 175 Third Street, part of the larger Gowanus Wharf campus in Brooklyn, the Commercial Observer reported. It’s the fifth building in the broader vision for the neighborhood. Apollo and Affinius Capital issued $600 million in debt for the project, representing the fourth development in Gowanus Wharf involving the latter party. The former has financed development in the neighborhood previously. Additionally, Scott Rechler’s RXR injected $185 million in […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Charney, Tavros, Incoco Capital close $785M (debt + equity) for 175 Third Street, Gowanus Brooklyn.
Apollo and Affinius Capital issue $600M debt; RXR injects $185M equity; 1,100 units, 27 stories, 1.1M sf.
Fifth Gowanus Wharf building; repeat lender/equity confidence signals neighborhood completion trajectory.
stories.summary: Charney Companies, Tavros, and Incoco Capital secured $785 million in financing for 175 Third Street in Gowanus Wharf, Brooklyn, the fifth building in the campus. Apollo and Affinius Capital provided $600 million in debt; Scott Rechler's RXR injected $185 million in equity. The 27-story, 1.1 million-square-foot property will include 1,100 residential units. RXR joined after the sponsors acquired the site from Aby Rosen's RFR, which had undertaken environmental remediation and secured a 421-a tax abatement.
stories.impact: Repeat institutional debt and equity commitment to Gowanus Wharf signals lender conviction in neighborhood trajectory and waterfront apartment demand. Developers should pursue adjacent Gowanus infill sites and waterfront packages; RXR and Affinius' participation validates Gowanus as a sustained capital destination, supporting elevated land valuations and feasibility for Class A mixed-income multifamily in the 15,000+ unit development zone.
